@ThinkingandTinkering you could also put/feed at some rate the magnet dust/metal/carbon loaded -self insulating- roll bar through one of those cheap £10 12V ZVS Low Voltage Induction Heating Power Supply Tesla Driver Board Module With Coil 
...& slowly cool it through a paint on heater tube to regenerate the metal crystal lattice oc.
 you would have to rezap it (as it cools!) with high dc to regain the curie point higher magnetic strength i suppose.
you did remember to put two separate copper wires in the center roll right ^_~ to make it easy to pull/zap/whatever on the imaginary diy table top/wall hanging -paper rolled bar+cotter pin quick assembly frame- cnc assembly line later

'Enhancing the bond strength in the meta-crystal lattice of ...'

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text:
Thoroughly enjoyed this project. It also hints at the versatility of paper as an engineering material (which was once more commonly used) for use in other projects. 
My gut reaction is that despite newspaper containing wood linguine which in time produces acids that makes the paper brittle. The fact that it is glued and thus preventing oxygen getting at the cellulose fibres, should give the bike a reasonable life. For even cheap metal bikes don’t last that long. 
Might be wise to have drain holes at the bottom of which ever parts are likely to pool condensation or seal those sections to stop all moister getting in. 
Acid free wall lining paper is maybe the better choice which is still inexpensive, stronger and should last longer. Yet, it certainly lacks the je ne sais quoi of The Sun! My choice would be the Guardian. Its long polysyllabic rambling paragraphs should help the bike glide over the deepest potholes.  Only  problem that remains is that I don’t know anyone that reads papers any more!

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text:
The japanese researched a lot about paper as a material for modern engineering. How it's folded or bended and other inspirations from origami and their classic architecture, all of this also influences the final result, besides the composition of the material.
Also wood derived materials such as super wood and maybe transparent wood have their lignin removed, maybe the same can be done here.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: It is not as inexpensive, but I would be more apt to use flax linen woven cloth. If you get a 50% off Joann's coupon, you can get it for reasonable, plus occasional online sales, clearances, etc.  Probably would use a cardboard tube to wrap it around. But then again, same issues of paper applies to the cardboard tube, so maybe not.  Maybe a bamboo garden stake pole or the like in that case (obviously one of the thicker ones).

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice post mate - I think when I do it again I am going to use Kraft paper

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: @justinw1765 Some natural fibers come braided into convenient cloths (woven in the case of flax), maybe the same can be done with paper.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
@vitordelima  Perhaps, but the individual fibers don't have near the tensile and other strengths of flax linen and hemp. 

  Exception being if you could make it out of something like tencel--a specialized rayon/wood cellulose fiber.

author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
text: I vagely remember that someone made some furniture out of old newspapers and cardboard. It was in some magazine (DIY type) , before internet and you tube. I've made a little stool out of cardboard , some years ago and it lasted for some years , before someone nicked it. Also made some stuff out of old clothes (glue laminations and some resin). For outdoor stuff , I have not found (cheap) solution for waterproofing and UV protection. Anyway , it was some decades ago and I since lost interest in that and the last idea was to make some terrace/patio furniture. Anyway it is a great stuff you are doing , as always.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: Titebond III wood glue is reasonably priced (at least in the US. I get a gallon for 28 dollars) and water resistant enough for most applications. Nor particularly affected by UV either, but if that is a worry, then a light coat of thinned silicone would greatly help (and further increase waterproofness).

author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
text:
Hey Robert, that's looking pretty good.  Very good, actually.  A few questions, though.

What weight does the frame alone come in at?  Did you do any testing as to the strength of the tubes, or are you just winging it?  I have a few ideas along these lines myself, and I missed the casein glue recipe you used, can you point me to it?

replies.author: @angelusmendez5084
replies.text:
It's in the video 1166.
Transcription of the recipe:

Casein based glue

Casein 100g
Water 150g
Calcium hydroxide 20g + water 50g
